items.q: What does specific latent heat measure?
items.correct: The energy required to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without changing its temperature.
items.misconception: The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg by 1 degree.
items.random_wrong: The temperature at which a substance melts or boils.
items.why: Students confuse specific latent heat with specific heat capacity because both involve 'heat' and 'kg', missing the no-temperature-change aspect of latent heat.

items.q: What happens to the total resistance when you add another resistor in parallel?
items.correct: Total resistance decreases — there are more paths for current to flow.
items.misconception: Adding any resistor always increases the total resistance.
items.random_wrong: Adding a parallel resistor doubles the voltage available.
items.why: Students apply the series rule (resistances add) to parallel circuits because they don't visualise multiple paths reducing the overall opposition.

items.q: What is the difference between energy and power?
items.correct: Energy is measured in joules; power is the rate of energy transfer, measured in watts (joules per second).
items.misconception: Energy and power are the same — both describe how much fuel something uses.
items.random_wrong: Power is the energy used over the lifetime of a device.
items.why: Students use 'power' in everyday language to mean 'a lot of energy', missing that power has the dimension of energy per time.

items.q: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1927. “1913” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Maximum binding energy per nucleon occurs around mass number:
items.why: Correct answer: 56. “12” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year Bohr published his hydrogen atom model?
items.why: The real figure is 1913 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
